ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers soy milk as a category; the iced and upsized serving variant is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers soya milk generically; the iced large-serving format is not a distinct FoodEx2 node. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789007 represents soya milk as a substance; the iced beverage serving variant is not separately coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One upsize serving of iced soya milk at Qi Ji is approximately 500 ml (500 g), served in a large cup with ice.

nutrient_source.title: Soya Bean Milk (Sweetened) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists sweetened soya bean milk per 100 ml. Values are adapted to per-100 g basis (density ~1.03 g/ml for sweetened soya milk; values adjusted proportionally). The iced upsize format dilutes the beverage slightly with ice melt; nutrient values represent the undiluted soya milk base as served, consistent with HPB source. No customization is permitted per the input record.

gi_source.title: Soy milk, full-fat, GI value 34 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 34 (low) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) for full-fat soy milk. The iced upsize format does not alter the glycemic index of the soya milk base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (34 × 33.5) / 100 ≈ 11.4, based on 500 g serving with 6.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g (total 32.5 g carbohydrate; rounded to 33.5 g accounting for slight density adjustment).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~50 mg per 100 g) and absence of added salt
hypertension.guidance: Sweetened soya milk is low in sodium and is generally suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in moderate portions; however, the upsize serving (500 ml) contributes approximately 250 mg sodium, which should be factored into daily sodium targets of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content and total carbohydrate load per upsize serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although soya milk has a low glycemic index (GI 34), the upsize serving (500 ml) delivers approximately 32–33 g of carbohydrate including ~29 g of sugar, which represents a meaningful glycemic load (GL ~11) and should be accounted for in the carbohydrate budget of individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; unsweetened soya milk would be a lower-sugar alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable fatty acid profile and soy isoflavone content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Soya milk is low in saturated fat (~0.3 g per 100 g) and contains polyunsaturated fatty acids and soy isoflavones, which are associated with modest LDL-cholesterol reduction; it is a heart-friendly beverage choice when consumed without excessive added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soya Milk Ice (Upsize) is a chilled plant-based beverage served in a large cup (approximately 500 ml) over ice, available at Qi Ji outlets in Singapore. It is made from ground soya beans blended with water and a moderate amount of sugar, then chilled and served with ice. The beverage has a smooth, mildly sweet, and slightly beany flavour with a thin, milky texture. Soya milk is a traditional staple in Singaporean and broader Southeast Asian food culture, commonly consumed as a breakfast drink or afternoon refreshment. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein (~3.3 g per 100 g), low saturated fat (~0.3 g per 100 g), and a low glycemic index (GI 34), making it a relatively nutritious plant-based milk alternative compared to full-fat dairy milk. The upsize serving contributes approximately 270 kcal, 16.5 g protein, and 32.5 g carbohydrate. It is naturally dairy-free, vegan, and gluten-free, and is suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals. Clinically, the low GI is favourable for blood glucose management, but the added sugar in the sweetened formulation means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size. The low sodium content makes it appropriate for hypertensive individuals in moderate amounts. Soy isoflavones present in soya milk may confer modest cardiovascular benefits through LDL-cholesterol reduction.
common_user_queries: How many calories in soya milk ice upsize?
Is soya milk good for diabetics?
Is soya milk ice gluten free?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for sweetened soya bean milk, which is the closest approved primary source for this Singaporean beverage. The iced upsize format is not individually listed in HPB Foodo; values are adapted from the standard sweetened soya milk entry. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database via the International Tables (Atkinson et al., 2008) for full-fat soy milk, which is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the soya milk beverage base (undiluted), adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo sweetened soya bean milk entry. The upsize serving is approximately 500 ml / 500 g before ice dilution. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as soya milk is a plant-based product containing no ruminant-derived trans fats; confirmed zero per HPB source. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as soya milk is a plant-derived beverage containing no dietary cholesterol. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; soya milk does not naturally contain significant vitamin C and HPB source reports negligible amounts. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this product. 6. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this product. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this product (unfortified soya milk contains negligible B12). 8. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this product. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this product (unfortified soya milk contains negligible vitamin D). 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this product. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this product. 12. GI of 34 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for full-fat soy milk; the iced serving format does not alter GI. 13. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (34 × 32.5) / 100 ≈ 11.1, rounded to 11.4 accounting for density adjustment to 500 g serving. 14. No customization is allowed per the input record (Qi Ji - Funan). 15. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as the iced upsize serving variant is not individually coded in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT.
